summary: Registry study of 288 primary malignant spine tumors in Japan (2006-2019); 5-year DSS was 47.5%, with osteosarcoma (21.9%) and chondrosarcoma (16.3%) most common.
main_conclusion: Chondrosarcoma (HR 0.23) and chordoma (HR 0.27) had better prognosis than osteosarcoma; advanced TNM stage was adverse (HR 7.60).

summary: Retrospective study of 181 CVJ lesion patients classified by dominant clinical syndrome: compression (54%) vs instability (46%), including extradural and intradural tumors.
main_conclusion: Dominant clinical syndrome-based classification enables appropriate surgical strategy selection for craniovertebral junction lesions.

summary: Pilot study of 38 patients with spinal metastasis treated by modified separation surgery combined with intraoperative radiotherapy (8-10 Gy); VAS and KPS improved significantly.
main_conclusion: Modified separation surgery with IORT is safe and effective for spine metastases with ESCC grade >=2 and SINS >=7.

summary: Retrospective study of 217 metastatic spine surgery patients comparing age groups; 90-day mortality was comparable (15% vs 13%, P=0.837) between elderly and younger patients.
main_conclusion: Age alone does not determine surgical outcomes; elderly patients achieve comparable short-term survival despite worse baseline neurological status.

summary: Retrospective analysis of 55 lung cancer spinal metastasis surgery patients; median survival 16.83 months, 1-year survival rate 58.18% with significant pain and function improvement.
main_conclusion: Visceral metastases and postoperative bone modifying agents are independent factors influencing 1-year survival after surgery.

summary: Case report and review of 68 NSCLC intramedullary spinal cord metastasis cases; cervical spine involved in 47.1%, surgery+chemotherapy subgroup mean OS 15.0 months.
main_conclusion: Multimodal therapy combining surgery and chemotherapy achieves favorable neurological and survival outcomes in cervical ISCM from NSCLC.

summary: Review of mobile spine and sacral chordoma management; en bloc resection is primary treatment but late recurrence occurs in up to 50% within 10-15 years.
main_conclusion: Multidisciplinary approach integrating surgery, advanced radiation (proton/carbon ion), and emerging immunotherapy is essential for chordoma.

summary: AO Spine review of fusion necessity in metastatic spine surgery; 4 key articles evaluated with expert consensus recommendations formulated.
main_conclusion: Fusion rarely influences outcomes in metastatic spine surgery; routine fusion attempts or delaying adjuvant therapy are not justified.

summary: Case report of T11 en bloc spondylectomy for solitary nasal adenocarcinoma metastasis using posterior approach with 3D CT navigation; disease-free at 52-month follow-up.
main_conclusion: En bloc spondylectomy provides excellent local control and durable disease-free survival in carefully selected solitary spinal metastases.

summary: Case of primary meningeal melanoma (T8-T12) with mixed intra/extramedullary components; gross total resection achieved via T7-T12 laminectomy with GNAQ mutation confirmed.
main_conclusion: Microsurgical gross total resection with neuromonitoring is feasible for rare mixed intramedullary-extramedullary meningeal melanomas.

summary: Case of hemicorporectomy for dedifferentiated sacral chordoma with extensive pelvic invasion; clear margins achieved but pulmonary metastases at 6 months, death at 9 months.
main_conclusion: Hemicorporectomy remains a viable last-resort option providing short-term survival and symptom relief in locally advanced pelvic tumors.

summary: Institutional series of 56 myxopapillary ependymoma patients over 23 years; en bloc GTR achieved in proximal filum tumors, CSF dissemination in 19.6%, median follow-up 126 months.
main_conclusion: En bloc resection is curative for small proximal filum MPEs; larger distal tumors have higher recurrence and CSF dissemination risk.

summary: Systematic review and meta-analysis of SRS for primary intradural spinal tumors (14 studies); short-term local control 98%, long-term local control 94%.
main_conclusion: SRS achieves favorable local control for primary intradural spinal tumors, with meningiomas showing the best response (LC 99%).

summary: Prospective multicenter study of 336 patients; 3-month mortality after metastatic spine surgery was 15.5%, with cancer progression causing 54% of deaths.
main_conclusion: High modified Glasgow Prognostic Score (AUC 0.716) and low preoperative performance status independently predict early postoperative mortality.

summary: Meta-analysis of 32 studies (1149 patients) comparing en bloc spondylectomy vs separation surgery with SBRT for spinal metastases; comparable survival and recurrence rates.
main_conclusion: En bloc spondylectomy and separation surgery with SBRT yield similar oncological outcomes, but en bloc has higher complication rates.

summary: Retrospective study of 43 patients (48 lesions) treated with donut-shaped circumferential CyberKnife SBRT; 1-year local control 87.1%, median OS 17 months.
main_conclusion: Donut-shaped circumferential CyberKnife SBRT achieves high local tumor control with minimal myelopathy risk in spinal metastases.

summary: Review of carbon fiber-reinforced PEEK spine implants as alternative to titanium in spine tumor surgery, enabling artifact-free postoperative imaging for radiation planning.
main_conclusion: CFR-PEEK implants reduce imaging artifacts on CT/MRI, improving radiation planning accuracy and recurrence detection in spine oncology.

summary: Long-term study of 23 TES patients (mean follow-up 11.5 years); instrumentation failure-free survival was 85.2% at 5 years and 65.7% at 10 years, local recurrence 13%.
main_conclusion: Rod fracture is a common late complication after TES; longer resection length increases instrumentation failure risk despite bony union.

summary: Two cases of mobile spine chordoma treated with separation surgery, CFR-PEEK instrumentation, and adjuvant SBRT; no recurrence at 18-month follow-up in both patients.
main_conclusion: Hybrid therapy with separation surgery, SBRT, and CFR-PEEK instrumentation is a viable strategy for mobile spine chordomas.

summary: Retrospective study of 33 mobile spine chordoma patients comparing en bloc resection vs intralesional surgery plus hadron therapy; no LC difference between groups.
main_conclusion: Intralesional surgery with adjuvant charged-particle therapy achieves comparable local control to en bloc resection for mobile spine chordomas.
